speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter-in-law, Robin Presser. Yes. And this is my husband, Bernard Presser. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my daughter-in-law, Frances-- Frances-- Frances Presser, Frances. And this is Danielle Presser, my granddaughter. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I first came to learn-- start learning about my grandparents' experience when, thinking about, year 10, my English teacher asked me to record, well, write an essay on three generations in my family. So that's my grandpa, or my grandma, and my dad, and me. And when I started to learn about-- [INAUDIBLE] I had to interview them on tape. And I started to learn all about the war-- basically just about the war, not actually the details of what happened to them, and things like that. It was pretty broad. And then I think that generated a lot of discussion, because then Danielle had essays to do. Josh had essays to do. Everyone was doing essays on history. And on my grandparents' experiences. And then when I fully started to get into it was when I had to a writing folio. And then I started asking more personal questions, because I was a bit more aware of what the war-- happened in World War II. And I kind of-- I was doing history at the time, too. And then I wrote my story about them. It was basically based on my grandma. And it taught me to be a bit more aware of what was going on in the world. I had really quite no idea that something like that had actually happened. And having them, and seeing them, is why I wrote the story, actually, because I want to remember my grandma. And I saw a lot of the old photos of how young they were, and how pretty, and just the life they used to have. That it could go from something like that, to what it actually-- when I saw the movie, in more visual terms, that kind of opened my eyes a bit. And I couldn't believe it. I just was like-- I didn't really-- didn't really realize it. And I still really can't. But I kind of just-- I don't really think about that much anymore, a lot, at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I've, like, throughout my whole childhood I went to a Jewish youth movement, [INAUDIBLE], and I was kind of bombarded every year at Holocaust Memorial, with all the terrible things that happened throughout the war. And so it was kind of pretty natural. I knew what happened. It was terrible. But not until my brother was in History in about year 10 that I actually knew what happened to my grandparents, when they started talking about it, when he did the interview with them. And I found out then that there were actually some good people in the war as well. And then I have found out more about righteous Gentiles, and how they were so lucky throughout it. And how it brought new light to it, that it wasn't full of bad people. And I seemed to look at the good side of it more. Whereas, everybody else was showing me the pictures of all the deaths and everything. Like it was there, but I was just so overwhelmed that they had-- throughout the war, they constantly told me how lucky they were the whole time. Like, luck just worked for them constantly. Every little thing that happened was working with them, not against them. And I think they're very special people because of that. Because obviously, that's what was meant to happen. Because it did. And they came through. And, and it's real-- like, lately they've been talking about it more and more. And I'm really happy to hear about it, because I know it helps them to talk about it. And I really want to know about it more and more. And it has-- they have been talking about it. And it must be hard for them to talk about it. But they-- we're really proud of them for doing it today, because now it's going to be documented in history. And everybody's going to have access to what I've had access to. So I'm really happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, before your grandchildren came, you were telling me about how you were treated in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you looked, compared to the other prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We looked very, very good. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that they wanted to take your blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They took it. It's when they take us, all of us, from Schindler's List. They took us to take it. I didn't know what they want. But they took blood. This was good for the German army, our blood. And I was so frightened to give the blood, you know. And I see this, this-- what they take the blood that I take the piece of cotton wool and I jump that day, and I never give them the blood, you know. When they see this, they can shot me. But, you know, when you're young, and so stupid, you do these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you pretended that it was already done.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I stepped. And later, they gave us a piece of sausage for this. But I I was-- I was-- I shouldn't do this, because it was really, they could shoot me, straight away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was the doctor taking the blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a few doctors. You know, I don't know these people. I, I never saw Mengele, thank god, but I heard about him. But-- and there were some doctors, German doctors. Yes. When I was over there, I met my neighbors, you know. And then she saw me. She was a Polish lady from the same house where we lived. She live in the ground floor. We live in the first floor. She was arrested because she listened to the wireless. You know, it was this time, they arrest Polish people. But she was in the-- she saw me. She was so happy. She saw. She said, I want to see you. I'll bring you some coats. She brought me two for my mother, a little coat for my sister and for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: A coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three coats, because we haven't got. I have a little frock to here, from some French girl or something. No panties, no nothing, no socks. And she bring everything for us. It was the most charming lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the coats came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people that were killed. You know, everything. When she was-- Polish people were treated better. And she was a long time over there. You know, because she was moving. I didn't even know where she was working. I don't know where she was working. But she have the potential to get these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is her name? I don't remember, sorry. I should remember her name. I met her after the war. She was alive. She live in the same house when we have the factory. Three sisters. God forbid, I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it will come to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Much older, like I was. She was much older.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing at Auschwitz? You mentioned this frock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when they took our things, they threw away to us whatever they have, you know, some clothes. They gave me the panties that I couldn't wear because they had blood all over. I threw them away. And they give me a little frock. I tell you, they left some French girl or something, because it was so small. Black frock, like a little one. Nothing else. No shoes, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a marking like a triangle, or a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. No, nothing. Nothing. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other prisoners had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in this. In Birkenau, where we had been. I told you, the Greek girls had been over there, what I noticed. And all of us in one barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the Greek girls wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal clothes, normal some, and whatever anybody had. They had been a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak Greek?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We only-- we didn't know what they were going. They paint their faces.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Polish language, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No German?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit German, maybe. A little bit German.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you stayed in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three weeks. I think two. My husband says longer. But this I should ask somebody, because I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact, or any news, from Schindler while you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We don't. We still think, what will be, what will be, because we don't know. When you have have-- when we have been treated like the normal prisoners, you know. The soup in the night time or in the-- we didn't know when is day, when is night. We didn't sleep. We didn't eat. You know, it was a house. They make this specially.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, they tell us to go to the-- they say, people who are in Schindler's list, out. We go out. And they, you know, my mother-in-law suddenly disappeared. We didn't know they, they-- the lady who was running this barrack, you know. She makes some money. She hide my mother-in-law. And she takes somebody else she wanted to try to give on the list, you know. She gets something for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we know, where is our mother? We started to make-- you know, we talked. Talk started. She's not here. She's not here. And when somebody come under her name, we say this is not her, you know. And they found my mother-in-law, and she came. Yes. She was-- she was still young. She was maybe-- maybe 45. But for us, she seems old. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 20. 20, 21, maybe. Maybe 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you if there were any children in Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me some more about how you left Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Auschwitz by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we know that we're going to Brinnlitz. This, we have been already happy. Yes. We know that we're going to Brinnlitz. And even I get-- the German soldiers have, I don't know, stay in the train with, with the-- in our-- our, not train only. Wagons. Yes. And when I get a big bread from one, and I say, what I do. With the bread, and Schindler, we don't need bread. We'll have bread. We have in Poland. And when I come, my husband was over there. It was snowing. Beautiful place, Brinnlitz. And I told him, what to do, I have bread to give somebody. No, keep this bread. Keep this bread. It was not as easy over there, like in Poland, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see your husband after Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came to the station. When we arrived, they come. Because we come to Schindler's place, because the train was going over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have been very happy. When I saw that my brother come too, and I want to give him this bread. My husband said, keep this bread. Keep this bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they look to you? The men?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men looked all right. Like they used to before. They stayed not long in Gross-Rosen, too. And they come over there. Over there was very good. I mean, not enough maybe, food. For me it was enough. My husband was hungry all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was snowing. It was winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Oh, which year. Which year it was-- wait a moment. It was nearly finished-- the war was finished in four months, I think. It was four--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a feeling that the war was ending?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we, we-- you know, the Germans told us to move. Because they have been-- not running away, yet. But they moving, you know, they move. Soon they liquidate the Plaszow and everything. They, they, they-- they run away slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any planes from the Allies?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any, or see any, planes? Airplanes from the Allies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One only when we have been in Schindler's place. One dropped to our Australian plane. They shot the Australian plane. And half of the back of the plane dropped to our camp with the pilot dead. But he was-- even somebody find $100 in with-- in this man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Australian pilot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Australian pilot, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know his name, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They make him a-- the Germans came and make him a really proper funeral, yes, with honors, with everything. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why you were going to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he liquidated this factory, and everything go over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Schindler set up a new factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see Schindler again after Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when we arrived. When we arrived. I don't remember this, exactly, but he was over there, I, I suppose. So I suppose. I don't remember him just this moment. I don't remember in this moment when we arrived. I saw my husband and my brother. The people who were in service, but he came later on, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Schindler say anything to you when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, didn't you trust me? You see you are here. He told us this, yes, it's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the factory already established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was everything nearly already. And we had barracks. In the old factory, we used to have-- they put only the, the beds for us. And it was-- it was nice and warm, beautiful. I cured my, my-- I was sick, you know. I had the inflammation of the-- here, this part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shoulder. Joint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this heating, what was over there. Was heating where the factory was. And was heating. And I was sleeping under it. Yes, the first floor I was sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by Schindler in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the same, very good. We have a lady who looked after us. German ladies. And when they took us to the toilet or something, you know, they took us. You can't go out by yourself. Only thing I want to-- the sun was able to. Oh, I want to stay a few minutes here. Oh no, it's not for you. You go over there. It's for us, is the sun. This was everything what I have with them. [INAUDIBLE]. But they couldn't do nothing to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: One lady even met the girl who was, before the war-- for she was the nurse for her child. You know. She was a chemist from Krakow. And she was very good. She always bring something to us. She was very good, fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with Schindler's wife, Mrs. Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was to go, because we made her a jumper. The jumper we make, I think, for months, maybe, because anytime I come and show her how much we made already, she gave me a piece of bread, a piece of some sausage, or [INAUDIBLE] think there was so much food in her place. She always give us something-- give me something. So we make this jumper. We make, we make. In the meantime, I make the jumper during the night. For one jumper, one bread. For the Polish, for the Czechoslovakian. From the pieces of wool like this. Because once, this was a wool factory over there. And we find the wool. And we put together. And we make the jumpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Czechoslovakia for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was four months, only four months. This is it? Five months. Maybe five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was business there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Business was not so good, because it was not the, the pots and pans he couldn't have nothing to sell. And they cost money, you know, to give 1,000 people food. And the Germans give it to him, but still, he didn't do nothing. He spent the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he spend on?

speaker: interviewee
text: For us, for us, he spent because he don't get much. I mean, we get the soup. I don't know whether they asked me, what we eat for breakfast. I don't remember. I don't know. But we were supposed to eat something. But we have a good soup. And it was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all of the Jews on Schindler's List make it back from Gross-Rosen and Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. One doctor only in Gross-Rosen. He left. Because one doctor, who was not on the list, I suppose-- young doctor. He told him, you go. I have my life. I live very well all life. And you go. And I stay here. He was a very doctor. What was his name. Doctor-- I have his name in my [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Bilbaum? Bilbaum? No? Bilbaum? No. What's his name? [INAUDIBLE], a very nice doctor. Very nice doctor. When you want to have a day off, or something, you feel ill, you come to him, old man. He say, you undressed. You have a day off. And the doctor who took his place was named?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Young Rubenstein. Doctor Rubenstein. He's alive, in America, might be in Israel. Yes. He thought he was a fantastic man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other people who weren't on the list that came back from the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody who was on the list come. Every-- the same in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, any extra people coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Extra, not. Only these people. Thousands of people. To give the food to thousands of people-- Schindler, the Germans don't give him as much money. No, no. It was not so easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Was liberation for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 8Th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler was leaving the camp. And he wanted my husband to go with him and our family. But we had been five people, you know. And my husband told him, sorry. I can't take my whole family with me. And I don't want to leave the family. And he went with a few people, with friends. He went, and he lived as the [INAUDIBLE] because was [INAUDIBLE]. It was obviously some Ukrainian or something. They have been very danger. And he leave us that we can save ourselves, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He left you guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Guns, yes. And a few German people, he said, you can do with them what you like, but I think they should go. And we let them all go from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: German-- not workers. The soldiers, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he say where he was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was going to Germany. Anyway. He was afraid to go by himself, you know, because he was in trouble. I told you. He was-- Germans don't like him because he saved Jewish people. He was not very happy. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me again, what happened on that day? After Schindler left.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Schindler left, we went to leave--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give him any-- sorry. Did you give him anything when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: A gift--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we haven't got nothing. He give us. He give us materials, somebody who has a magazine. He give us some materials. And we haven't got nothing to give him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can we give him? Nothing. We stay in the place. When the porters leave. We leave together, with my brother, with my mother-in-law. My sister-in-law. A little room, next to the factory. We move over there after the war. After the war, I mean-- this when we have been-- when they, they come and they let us go. After liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a scene in the movie where the Jews take the gold from their teeth to make a ring. I'm just wondering if that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gets some ring, I think. But not in the last moment. Maybe he gets this-- you know, I don't remember, as a matter of fact. I was in the field--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no gold from your teeth taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I haven't got gold in my teeth. I have good teeth. Once, I went to the dentist, I have a pain in my teeth. And I went up to the Plaszow. They took me there. A Ukrainian took me over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, they thought that I ran away. What's happened? Did she run away? When you ran away, they shot you. I said, no, to the dentist. And I wanted-- this tooth was no good. They take the other one. But it was nothing. So how long did you stay in Czechoslovakia after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, maybe, a few weeks. We ate a little bit, you know. My husband, when they bring a little peaks like this, a small one. And my mother-in-law was cooking. And she slowly gave us food, because, you know, she was worried that we would be sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Russian soldiers tell you when they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I am [INAUDIBLE], but don't tell to the-- I mean, I don't know how he's speaking here Russian, but I said, don't tell, because I don't want my people to know, because they'd shoot me in my back. He was Jewish. This soldier. Yes. He was Jewish. He was a captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Sorry. I didn't ask him. Because we, we speak, you know, like his hand, but he said, I am [INAUDIBLE]. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he tell you would be happening to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He said, you can do what you like. That's it. We went-- we stayed over there a few weeks, I think. And we went back to Krakow. People go to America, people go everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of the Jews from Schindler's factory go back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Quite a lot, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel together?

speaker: interviewee
text: By lorry. You know, open train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We take whatever we can take, and we go. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find when you got back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, somebody was on this station. And he was sure that it's my brother and my husband's brother, because they looked like. And he say he was working once in his place, or something. He say, Mr. Janek. Janek. Janek. Janek, because his name was Janek. He said, oh, you should come to our place. And we went to their place. We stayed maybe two days in their place. And later, we go-- he-- went went to the city-- to the city. And we met some family of my husband. And we stayed with them. They already stayed in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was at your old house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody was living over there. But we had the factory back, after a while. Because somebody was over there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took over the factory during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- no, Germans they had been before. Later, somebody else was taking, because they think we are not in. We are not alive. And we go through the court. And then we get this back. And we have the flat, a nice flat. We pay for the flat, because you can, it was very hard. We take one room in this flat first. And later on, we get another one. We paid the other man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jews in Krakow after the war? After liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of come, all them, of people. Yes. A lot of people come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any help for you, for-- as a survivor? Was there any Allied organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't go for that even, because my husband started to work. We stayed with the family. They already had been here. You know, and we don't get nothing from anybody. We started from scratch, by ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Krakow after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 Years. 15 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, until 1959. Yes? Did you experience any anti-Semitism after the war, in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tell you not, because, I don't know. We live in the place when not many Jews lived. I don't know. I never had a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, when my son was maybe seven or eight, or seven. He started-- somebody, the boy from the street, he started to, Jew, Jew, to him, you know. And he was sure that he played with him. He didn't know that he is a Jew, because, you know-- and he said-- and they run around, and he, this boy, and he didn't know what it is. You know, my son didn't know. But it was nothing, really. No, never, I never go through-- we have a business. A very good business. And we lived very good-- too good to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we say-- my husband said, oh, you want me in the jail, or you want to live? I haven't got the-- I should live. Why would he go to jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we have been, we go against the government. You know, it was a Communist country. You couldn't have a private business. Whatever you do, it had been against them. And one day, they'd come and they'd arrest him. And we left before it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any plan of where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to go to Israel or to Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was not the army. I was afraid my kids go to the army. I don't like the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any family or friends in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my sister-in-law, she went with a lot of friends, a lot of friends. My sister-in-law was here, and a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop and change the tape.
